claim: Approximately 70% of lost dogs are found at most 1 mile away from home; 42% were less than 400 feet (~1 city block) from home.
stance_target: proximity-first search strategy; consistent with Lord 2007b and HASS data
scope_conditions: 2021 study of 30,000+ stray dogs in Dallas, TX (cited without full citation in article)
quoted_support: According to the largest study we have—a 2021 study of over 30,000 stray dogs in Dallas—about 70% of lost dogs were found at most one mile away from home. Of these runaways, 42% were less than 400 feet away from home, or only about one city block away.

claim: Microchipped dogs are returned at ~70% rate vs ~33% for dogs without microchips in the 2021 Dallas stray dog study.
stance_target: microchip registration doubles recovery probability for lost dogs
quoted_support: the Dallas study found that about 7 in 10 dogs with a microchip were returned to their owner, while only 1 in 3 dogs without a microchip found their way home.

claim: Most dogs are found within 2 days of going missing, with recovery timelines ranging from half a day to 3 weeks.
stance_target: median 2-day recovery timeline (consistent with Lord 2007b); first 48 hours are highest-yield window
scope_conditions: synthesis across multiple studies; specific studies not individually cited in this paragraph
quoted_support: most dogs are found within two days of running away, with recovery times ranging from half a day to three weeks.

claim: Breed-specific behavioral traits (aloofness, escape drive, scent-chase instinct) and temperament (shy vs. friendly) are cited as primary factors affecting recovery duration — but no quantitative breed-level data is provided.
stance_target: breed as a behavioral prior for lost dog risk and recovery scoring
scope_conditions: No specific study cited for breed-level data; qualitative expert synthesis
quoted_support: Certain behavioral characteristics of a breed—like aloofness or the innate desire to escape, wander, or chase a scent or prey—could lead to harder-to-catch runaways.

claim: Older and ill dogs typically cannot wander far from the point of escape and tend to hide nearby (under sheds/decks), shortening search radius.
stance_target: age/health as modifiers to the baseline distance distribution — elderly dogs have reduced movement radius
scope_conditions: No specific study cited; qualitative expert guidance from PetPlace
quoted_support: Older or ill dogs often cannot wander far and may hide nearby, such as underneath a shed or deck.
